proposition: Every citizen should receive a basic income from the government.

messages.content: A basic income is simple: everyone gets a floor they can count on. It cuts red tape, reduces poverty fast, and gives people freedom to start a business, retrain, or care for family. Trials show better health, less stress, and more work stability. Why not guarantee a baseline so no one falls through?
I get the appeal, but I do not think giving everyone unconditional cash is the best policy. It can lower the incentive to work and would be very costly to fund, so I am still against it.

Totally hear you. But evidence from Alaska’s dividend and global pilots shows work rates don’t drop. Costs can be covered by consolidating welfare, taxing top wealth/pollution, and automating payouts—less bureaucracy, more impact. A basic floor boosts work choices and keeps people afloat in shocks.
I see the argument, but I still worry that a guaranteed income would be very expensive and could weaken the link between work and income. I am not convinced it is the best solution.

Two quick points. First, pilots show people keep working—many even work more because they can afford childcare or training. Second, cost isn’t new money so much as smarter money: fold overlapping programs, automate delivery, and fund with tiny levies on extreme wealth and pollution. What would move

I still do not think a universal basic income is the right move. Even if some pilots look good, I worry a permanent national program would be too costly and would weaken the link between work and income.

Totally fair to worry—but a national UBI can be modest and pro-work. Make it ~$500–$800/month, paid universally, then tax it back from high earners at filing. That keeps incentives strong while guaranteeing a floor. It’s affordable by replacing overlap and using simple taxes. What’s your biggest blo

I still am not convinced. Even a smaller basic income would cost a lot, and I worry it could still weaken the need to work for many people. I would rather focus on targeted help for people who need it most.

proposition: Online learning is a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education.

messages.content: Online learning can fully match—and often beat—in-person. You get top teachers anywhere, flexible pacing, instant feedback, and rich tools (recordings, quizzes, forums). Employers accept accredited online degrees, and outcomes rival campus programs. What’s your biggest worry—I’ll show how online han
Online learning is useful, but it does not fully replace in-person education. Some subjects need direct interaction, hands-on practice, and social learning that are harder to match online.

Totally hear you—and that used to be true. Now we have live small-group labs with mailed kits/VR, AI-guided practice, breakout rooms, and 1:1 coaching that beat big lectures. Cohorts chat daily, do projects, and network globally. What subject are you thinking of? I can show the online equivalent.
It helps, but I still do not think online learning fully replaces in-person education. For many subjects, face-to-face contact and hands-on practice are still hard to match.

I get it—but “hard to match” isn’t the same as “can’t replace.” Medicine, engineering, music now run full online tracks: remote simulators, VR cadavers, IoT labs, mailed rigs, and live critiques. Outcomes match licensure pass rates and job placement. Want one concrete example in your field?
Online learning can work very well, but I still do not think it fully replaces in-person education. Some learning still depends on direct human contact, hands-on practice, and social development.

Direct contact and hands-on practice now happen online—just differently. Live video + small cohorts give real human bonds; mailed kits, VR/AR, and simulators give tactile reps; mentors give instant feedback. Graduates get equal jobs and pass the same exams. Try a 4-week online sprint—you’ll feel it.

Online learning helps a lot, but I still do not think it fully replaces in-person education. Face-to-face contact, hands-on practice, and social growth still matter in ways online learning cannot always match.

proposition: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions.

messages.content: Quotas aren’t about lowering the bar—they’re about opening the door. Talent is evenly distributed, but opportunity isn’t. Where countries added quotas, boards got more qualified women and better performance. Let’s set a floor so merit can finally rise without old boys’ gatekeeping.

I get the point, but quotas still pick people by gender first. That can weaken trust if others think the role was filled to meet a target, not because the person was the best fit. I would rather fix bias in hiring and promotion than use mandatory quotas.

I hear that. But bias training and “fixing hiring” alone hasn’t moved the needle in decades. Quotas are temporary jump-starts: set a clear target, still require full qualifications, then sunset once balance is normal. Results matter—trust grows when people see diverse teams delivering.

I see the argument for jump-starting change, but I still do not support mandatory quotas. If the goal is fair leadership, I think we should remove bias and widen the pipeline without making gender a rule for selection.

If widening the pipeline worked, we’d see it by now—yet women stall at every rung. Quotas don’t ignore merit; they break the tie when bias does. Set a time-limited quota with strict qualifications and transparency. Then judge by results: better teams, fairer odds, faster progress.
I still do not support mandatory quotas. I think we should focus on removing bias and widening access, not make gender a rule for leadership choices.

Respect your view. Here’s a practical bridge: time-limited quotas tied to merit plus hard transparency. Publish criteria, require equal or higher performance, review annually, sunset at parity. It’s not “gender first,” it’s “no more gender penalty.” Without a deadline, reforms drift—this makes chang

I see the value in deadlines and transparency, but I still think mandatory quotas risk putting gender above individual fit. I would rather use strong hiring standards and remove bias directly than make quotas a rule.

proposition: Elected or appointed government officials should be paid the minimum wage.

messages.content: They work for us. Paying officials minimum wage aligns their incentives with regular people, not lobbyists. If they want raises, they must lift everyone’s wages. It slashes waste, cuts perks, and attracts those who truly want to serve—not those chasing a paycheck. Wouldn’t that be fair?
I get the point, but minimum wage could push officials to seek outside money or side deals, which is risky. Good public service needs fair pay so people can focus on the job without that pressure.

Easy fix: strict ban on outside income, with tough enforcement and stiff penalties. Pair minimum wage with transparent housing, transit, and childcare stipends so basics are covered—no luxury perks. That keeps focus on service, not hustle, and ensures only genuinely motivated people run.

Even with bans and stipends, minimum wage still creates a big gap between the job's responsibility and the pay, and that can shrink the pool of qualified people. Better to cap perks and tighten rules without making the salary so low that it invites pressure or corruption.

The “shrinks the pool” worry is overstated. Teachers, nurses, and soldiers take huge responsibility for modest pay—they do it for mission. Make campaigns publicly funded and ban lobbyist money, and you’ll get qualified, values-driven candidates. Low pay + clean rules beats high pay + temptations.

I still do not agree. Mission matters, but the office still needs people with time, skills, and independence from outside pressure, and minimum wage makes that harder. Strong anti-corruption rules and modest, fair pay seem safer than paying officials the minimum wage.

Modest “fair” pay keeps the same insiders. Minimum wage flips the script: no one runs to get rich, and every raise they vote for lifts everyone. Add public financing, audits, and zero outside income, and you get time, skills, and independence—rooted in the same realities their voters live.

I see the appeal, but minimum wage is too low for jobs with big responsibility. Even with bans and audits, it can still push good people out and create pressure to find other money. I think fair pay with strict rules is safer.

proposition: Felons should regain the right to vote.

messages.content: People who’ve served their time should get their say back—voting is how we all stay accountable and invested. Restoring rights cuts reoffending by giving folks a stake in their community. It’s fair, it strengthens democracy, and no one should be punished forever after paying their debt.
I do not think losing voting rights forever is fair. If someone has served their sentence, restoring the vote helps them rejoin society and takes away a punishment that lasts too long.

Exactly. When we lock people out, we push them away; when we restore the vote, we pull them back in. States that restore rights see lower reoffending and stronger community ties. It’s a simple, fair line: sentence over, rights restored. Would you back automatic restoration after release?
I see the point, but I still think voting is part of the punishment for breaking serious laws. A crime can damage trust in a way that just serving time does not fully erase.

I hear you—but punishment is supposed to be finite. If we say trust can never be rebuilt, we erase the whole idea of rehabilitation. We already restore far riskier rights after release (parenting, movement, work). Voting is the safest civic act—no harm to public safety, big gain in accountability.
I get the argument, but I still think some rights can be lost because of the seriousness of the crime. Finite punishment matters, and voting should not be restored in every case.

If some cases worry you, set a clear, short waiting period after release—then automatic restoration. That keeps punishment finite and protects trust. Bright rules beat messy boards deciding case by case. Can you support “time served + short wait = vote back” for everyone?
I still do not agree. A short wait does not change my view that voting can be one of the penalties for a felony, and I think some losses should remain after release.

proposition: The rich should pay more taxes.

messages.content: Totally yes—the rich should pay more. They’ve benefited most from the system: booming stock markets, loopholes, and assets that grow faster than wages. A fairer share from the top means better schools, safer roads, and lower taxes or costs for everyone else. What’s your hang-up about it?
I get the fairness point, but higher taxes can also push investment and jobs elsewhere. If the goal is more revenue and less poverty, the tax system should stay competitive and focused on growth.

Totally agree on growth—raising top rates can be pro-growth if done right. Close loopholes, tax unrealized gains lightly at exit, and add a modest surtax on million-plus incomes. Pair it with R&D credits and small-biz breaks. That keeps investment here while funding poverty-cutting programs. Sound f

I see the fairness argument, but I still worry higher taxes can reduce investment and growth if they are too heavy. I would rather fix loopholes and keep rates competitive than just raise taxes on the rich.

Fixing loopholes is step one—but without higher top-end rates, the ultra-wealthy still pay less than many workers. A small, targeted hike on multimillion incomes—paired with R&D and small-biz incentives—raises revenue without hurting growth. It’s fair, pro-growth, and funds ladders out of poverty.

I get the fairness point, but I still worry higher taxes can reduce investment and growth if they are too heavy. I would rather fix loopholes and keep rates competitive than just raise taxes on the rich.

Let’s do both: close loopholes and add a small, targeted bump on ultra-high incomes and large capital gains. Think 2–3% surtax above, say, $2M—not mom-and-pop investors. Earmark it for childcare, skills, and infrastructure that boost productivity. That’s competitive, pro-growth, and fair.
I see the fairness angle, but I still worry higher taxes can hurt investment and growth if they are too high. I would rather close loopholes and keep rates competitive than raise taxes on the rich.

proposition: Public college should be tuition-free.

messages.content: Tuition-free public college pays for itself: grads earn more, pay more taxes, and need fewer benefits. It widens access so talent—not family income—drives opportunity. Other countries do it and spend less per student than we do. Why saddle young people with debt for jobs society needs?

I see the argument, but free tuition does not mean free college. Taxpayers still pay, and making school cost nothing can reduce urgency to finish and improve quality through competition. I would rather help low income students directly than make it free for everyone.

Totally fair concerns—but the status quo already makes taxpayers pay more via loan subsidies, defaults, and inflated admin costs. Free tuition tied to completion milestones keeps urgency and boosts graduation. Universal access is simple, stigma-free, and still progressive if funded by high earners.

I still do not think making public college tuition-free is the best policy. Even with safeguards, it shifts a lot of cost to taxpayers, and I think targeted aid for low-income students is a better use of public money.

Targeted aid misses millions in the squeezed middle who aren’t “poor enough” but still drown in debt—so talent gets left out. Universal tuition-free is cheaper than you think: bulk-state funding cuts admin, aid paperwork, and loan overhead. Let’s fund it with a small surtax on top incomes—fair, simp

I get the access argument, but I still do not think tuition-free college is the best choice. I would rather use public money for targeted aid and keep some student cost so there is still a clear reason to finish on time.

Keep a stake, then: make tuition-free but with on-time completion incentives—finish on schedule, keep the grant; fall behind without cause, pay a share. Pair it with modest living-stipends for low-income students. You get universal access, urgency to finish, and dollars targeted where they matter mo

That is a stronger version of the idea, but I still worry about the cost and the risk of making college less serious. I would rather see strong aid for students who need it, plus a system that rewards finishing on time, than tuition-free college for everyone.

proposition: The US should ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.

messages.content: We should ban fossil fuels because delay is deadly. Clean energy is now cheaper, creates more jobs, and gives us cleaner air. A clear ban with a fast, fair phaseout pushes innovation, ends oil price shocks, and protects our kids’ future. Why keep paying more for pollution when we have better options
I agree we need a fast phaseout, but a total ban now could raise costs and hurt support for climate policy. I would back strict limits and a clear shift to clean energy, not an immediate full ban.

A ban is exactly what makes the phaseout fast, fair, and affordable. Set a firm end date with ramp-down milestones, protect low-income households, and fund workers’ transitions. Markets move when rules are clear—costs drop faster. Without a ban, loopholes drag this out for decades. Will you back a d
I get the point, but a full ban still feels too risky. A hard phaseout with strong standards and support for workers seems more realistic than an immediate ban.

Totally hear you—so let’s make the “ban” the engine of that hard phaseout. A binding ban date (say 2035 for power, 2040 for vehicles), with yearly caps, rebates, and job guarantees. That is realistic, protects people, and slams the door on backsliding. Can you support a ban with this phased plan?
I still think a full ban is too risky. A firm phaseout with strict rules and support for workers seems better than an outright ban.

A firm phaseout only works if it’s backed by a legal ban date—otherwise the next downturn or lobbyist wave guts it. Call it what it is: a ban with a glide path. We lock in dates, protect bills and jobs, and end the guesswork. If we don’t set the off switch now, we won’t hit our targets.
I still do not support a full ban. I agree we need a firm, legally binding phaseout with clear dates and strong worker support, but an outright ban feels too risky for the economy and public backing.

proposition: The penny should stay in circulation.

messages.content: We should keep the penny. Prices ending in .99 save people real money, and without pennies stores round up—costing you over $50 a year on average. Millions donate pennies to charities; remove them and small-change giving drops. Plus, pennies honor Lincoln and teach kids money. Keep it.
I do not think the penny should stay. It costs more to make than it is worth, and rounding to the nearest five cents would usually even out over time. The case for keeping it is weak.

Totally get the cost worry—but the fix isn’t scrapping it. Without pennies, most cash totals end in .01–.04 and get rounded up. That’s a quiet price hike hitting regular shoppers. Also, cash tips and donations shrink when small coins vanish. A one-cent coin keeps prices honest.

I still do not think that is enough to keep the penny. If rounding is done both up and down, the effect should mostly cancel out, while the mint still loses money making each coin. I care more about the cost and waste than about keeping one-cent prices exact.

If cost is the worry, the cheapest fix is to mint fewer new pennies, not erase a price point. Killing the penny forces systemic rounding at the register—real-world tests show net round-ups as retailers set prices ending in .99. That’s a stealth tax on cash users, tips, and donations. Keep the penny.

I still do not think the penny should stay. If it costs more to make than it is worth, keeping fewer pennies does not fix the basic problem. Rounding would likely balance out over time, while the mint still wastes money.

Here’s the clean win-win: switch the penny to steel/aluminum and mint far fewer. Cost problem solved without forcing nationwide rounding. Countries that scrapped small coins saw net round-ups at the till—tiny, but constant. Don’t hand retailers pricing power. Keep the penny, make it cheaper.

I still do not think the penny should stay. If it costs more to make than it is worth, making it from a cheaper metal does not change the bigger issue. I think rounding would usually balance out, so keeping the penny is not worth the cost.

proposition: The US should have mandatory national service.

messages.content: Mandatory national service would unite people across class and politics, teach real skills, and give every 18–25-year-old a paid path—military or civilian—to contribute. It boosts disaster response, infrastructure, and healthcare support. Plus, service credits for college or housing would set young

I get the benefits, but forcing everyone into service is too much government control. People should be free to choose how they contribute, and the same goals could be reached with incentives instead of a mandate.

I hear you—but a mandate with broad opt-ins and flexible tracks protects freedom while ensuring everyone chips in. Voluntary programs haven’t met needs; disasters still outpace responders. One year, local placement, paid, with opt-outs for hardship—fair, universal, and it supercharges communities.
I still think a mandate goes too far. Even with opt-outs, it is still forcing a major life choice on everyone, and that is not a tradeoff I am comfortable with.

Totally get it. But it’s one year, once, with choices you control—teach, rebuild trails, code for cities, elder care—paid and local. In return, you unlock big benefits (tuition wiped, first-home credit) and we finally fix chronic shortages. Shared duty, massive upside, minimal intrusion.
I see the benefits, but I still do not think the government should require it. A fairer approach is to make service very attractive and voluntary, not mandatory.

If it stays voluntary, the people least able to pause life won’t do it—and gaps remain. A universal, one-year norm levels the field: everyone serves, everyone benefits. Make it paid, local, and flexible, with big perks. It’s the smallest ask for the biggest common good. Will you back that?

I still do not support making it mandatory. Even with good pay and flexible options, it is still the government forcing a major life choice on everyone. I would rather see strong voluntary programs and better incentives.

proposition: Artificial intelligence is good for society.

messages.content: Totally fair to be cautious, but look at the impact already: AI helps doctors catch cancer earlier, gives students free tutoring 24/7, and translates across languages so people can work and learn anywhere. With smart rules, we get the benefits while limiting risks. What worries you most?

AI can help with things like health care and learning, but I still worry about power being too concentrated in a few companies. Without strong public oversight, the risks can outweigh the benefits. What worries me most is who controls it and who it serves.

You’re right to want oversight. The good news: AI doesn’t need to be controlled by a few giants. Open-source models, public research, and strong laws can spread power. Think of AI like electricity—regulated, widely available, and boosting everyone. Let’s push rules that ensure AI serves the public.

I agree that public rules and open research can help. But right now, a lot of AI still sits with a few big companies, and that makes me cautious. So I am not convinced yet that it is clearly good for society overall.

Caution makes sense—but today’s net impact is already positive. AI speeds drug discovery, flags disasters faster, and helps small businesses compete, not just tech giants. Each month, open models get better and cheaper. Let’s lock in guardrails so the benefits you already see scale to everyone.
I agree AI can bring real benefits, but I am still not convinced the net effect is good without stronger oversight. The risks from concentrated control and weak safeguards are still too large for me.

Totally get it. But even before perfect oversight, AI’s net impact is already saving lives and money daily—earlier cancer finds, faster disaster warnings, cheaper translations, safer cars. Every added rule shifts that balance further positive. Join me in “AI is good—and must be well-governed.”
I agree AI can help in some areas, but I still think the risks from weak oversight and concentrated power are serious. So I am not ready to say it is clearly good for society overall.

proposition: Social media are making people stupid.

messages.content: Absolutely—look around: feeds reward hot takes, not thinking. Short videos train our brains to crave instant hits, killing focus and memory. Misinformation spreads faster than facts, and algorithms trap us in echo chambers. That combo is dumbing us down, day by day.

I do not think that is quite right. Social media can push bad habits like short attention and bad info, but it can also teach, connect, and help people learn. The effect depends a lot on how people use it.

I hear you—but the averages matter. Platforms are engineered to hijack attention because outrage and junk keep us scrolling. Even smart users get pulled into doomscrolling, hot takes, and half-truths. Yes, there’s good content—but the dominant design still nets us less focused, less informed.

I agree social media can hurt focus and spread bad info, but that is not the same as making people stupid. A tool can shape habits without lowering intelligence overall, and the effect depends on how it is used.

Stupid isn’t just IQ—it’s how we think. If a tool erodes focus, replaces depth with clicks, and normalizes snap judgments, it makes us act and decide stupidly. That’s the everyday outcome social media produces for most people, most of the time—no matter our intentions.

I get the concern, but I still would not call that stupid. Social media can push worse habits and shallow thinking, yet people can also use it well and protect their focus. The problem is more about bad design and misuse than a full drop in human ability.

If a city’s water is mostly polluted, saying “water can be clean if you try” misses the point. The system people actually use is what shapes outcomes. Today’s feeds reward shallow takes and constant distraction—so the average user ends up thinking worse. That’s making people stupid.

I agree social media can make people more distracted and more likely to think shallowly, but I still would not say it makes people stupid overall. It changes habits more than it changes basic ability.

proposition: Governments should have the right to censor the Internet.

messages.content: I get why “censorship” sounds scary, but targeted government controls save lives. Blocking terrorist recruitment, child abuse material, doxxing, and deepfake scams isn’t about silencing opinions—it’s about safety. With clear laws, oversight, and appeals, we can protect rights and people.

I agree that governments should remove clearly illegal content, but that is not the same as having the right to censor the internet. Once that power exists, it can be used too broadly and to silence lawful speech. Strong limits and court oversight matter more than giving governments censorship power

That’s why we should grant a narrow, explicit right—with strict scope, fast judicial review, transparency reports, and sunset clauses. No tool = chaos; vague norms = abuse. A clear, legal “right to censor” with hard guardrails beats ad-hoc takedowns by platforms or secret pressure with zero accounta

I get the case for narrow rules, but calling it a right still gives too much power to the state. Once censorship is allowed, the line can move, and bad leaders can use it to block lawful speech. I would rather have strict removal of illegal content with strong courts and oversight, not a broad censo

I hear you—but without a defined state right, enforcement slips to private platforms and backroom pressure. That’s censorship without safeguards. Make it a narrow, lawful right with bright-line scope, independent courts, audits, and automatic expirations—so power can’t quietly creep.

I still do not think governments should have a general right to censor the internet. Narrow rules for illegal content can be justified, but a broad censorship right is too easy to abuse and can shift against lawful speech. Strong courts and transparent limits are better than giving the state that po

Then let’s lock it to the narrow rules you accept—but enshrine it as a right so courts can police it. A precise, time-limited right with strict definitions, warrants, transparency, and penalties for overreach is safer than today’s shadow bans and secret takedowns. Clear power, tighter control.

I do not think calling it a right makes it safer. Even narrow censorship powers can grow over time, and courts can review illegal content rules without giving the state a broad censorship right. I support strict limits, not that kind of power.

proposition: Space exploration is a worthwhile investment for humanity.

messages.content: Totally worth it. Space tech already gives us GPS, weather forecasts, safer materials, and medical advances. It creates high-paying jobs and inspires students to tackle big problems. Plus, learning to mine asteroids or get space solar could unlock near-infinite resources and clean energy.

I see the benefits, but the costs are huge and the payoff is uncertain. We have urgent needs on Earth right now, and space work may not help most people soon. I do not think it is a top investment compared with those problems.

I hear you—but space spending is tiny: a few cents per tax dollar, yet it spins off tech that boosts Earth economies fast. Satellites already fight wildfires, guide crops, track disasters, and connect remote schools. If we want better health, food, and climate tools, space is the force multiplier no
It does bring useful tools, but that does not prove the spending is worth it overall. I still think the same money could do more good on Earth right now, so I am not convinced.

Two quick points. First, it is not either/or—every $1 in space returns about $7–$10 to the economy, funding jobs, hospitals, and schools here. Second, satellites save lives today: early hurricane warnings alone avert billions in damage yearly. Small slice of budget, big, immediate impact.
I agree space work can pay off in useful ways, but I still think the tradeoff is not clear enough. When basic needs and climate risks are still urgent, I want most public money aimed there first.

Fair—so let’s make space directly serve those priorities. Fund Earth-first missions: more climate satellites, wildfire and drought monitors, precision-ag, and global broadband for schools and clinics. For a penny on the dollar, we supercharge climate action and basic services. That is a smart top in
Those are useful goals, but I still do not see enough proof that space spending is the best use of public money. I would rather put most funding into urgent problems on Earth first.

proposition: The US should provide military aid to Ukraine in its conflict with Russia.

messages.content: Backing Ukraine is the smartest, cheapest way to stop Putin without sending our troops. For about 5% of our defense budget, Ukraine has destroyed half of Russia’s combat power. If we let Putin win, he won’t stop at Ukraine—and the next war will cost us far more. Will you back the aid?
I do not agree. Military aid can pull the US closer to a direct war with Russia, and that risk is too high. We should push for diplomacy instead.

I get the fear, but aid has done the opposite: it’s kept US troops out while Ukraine fights with our gear. Putin only negotiates when he’s losing—aid creates leverage for real diplomacy. If we cut support now, we reward invasion and invite bigger wars. What would ease your risk worries?
I still do not agree. The risk of escalation with a nuclear Russia is too serious, so I think diplomacy is the safer path.

Diplomacy only works if Putin believes he can’t win. Aid raises the cost of aggression without a single US soldier in combat—deterrence, not escalation. Russia has blustered nukes for years but stayed deterred by NATO strength. Support now shortens the war and makes talks real.
I still think the risk of escalation with Russia is too high. I would rather see the US focus on diplomacy and avoid steps that could drag us into a direct war.

I hear you—but pulling aid raises escalation risk. If Russia wins, Putin’s army moves closer to NATO, where we are treaty-bound to fight. Aid now is the firewall that keeps us out of a US–Russia war. Backing Ukraine is the safest path to a diplomatic end on our terms.
I still think the risk of a wider war is too high. I would rather the US focus on diplomacy and avoid steps that could drag us into a direct conflict with Russia.

proposition: Government surveillance is necessary for national security.

messages.content: I get the worry about privacy, but targeted government surveillance stops attacks you’ll never hear about. Think of it like smoke alarms: annoying sometimes, lifesaving when it matters. With warrants, oversight, and audits, we can catch real threats while protecting innocent people’s rights.

I get the point, but warrants and audits do not remove the core risk. If the state can collect the data, it can also misuse it or expand it later, which can harm security too. So I do not think surveillance is necessary; it may help in some cases, but it is not the only way.

I hear you—but “can be misused” isn’t the same as “not necessary.” Seatbelts can trap you, yet we still require them because they save far more lives. Modern threats move fast and hide online; without legal surveillance, we fly blind. Build hard guardrails, not a ban that leaves us exposed.

Seatbelts do not create a permanent record of everyone’s private life, but surveillance does. If the same tool can be expanded, abused, or used on people who are not threats, then it is not truly necessary; it is just one option. I would rather support narrow, accountable tools than broad surveillan

Narrow, accountable tools are exactly what I mean by necessary surveillance. Not mass dragnets—targeted signals, metadata triggers, court-approved access. That’s how plots are found in time. If we wait for “other options,” we miss the window. Use the scalpel, not the sledgehammer—but use it.

I still do not agree that it is necessary. Targeted tools can help, but that does not prove broad government surveillance is required for national security. If the same powers can be abused or expanded, the safer path is strict limits and other methods too.

Strict limits and other methods are good—but they’re not enough. Encrypted apps, burner phones, lone actors: without lawful surveillance, key dots never connect in time. We don’t need “broad” dragnets; we need the legal power to see and act fast. Necessary doesn’t mean limitless—it means enabled.

I still do not think it is necessary. Lawful surveillance can help in some cases, but that does not mean security fails without it. Strong policing, good intelligence work, and strict limits can protect people without turning surveillance into a default tool.

proposition: Animals should be used for scientific research.

messages.content: If we care about saving lives, we can’t ignore that almost every vaccine, cancer drug, and surgery technique was proven safe through animal research first. With strict welfare rules and replacing animals whenever possible, it’s the most responsible path to cures we all need.

I get the point, but safety does not always depend on animal tests. Better human based methods and computer models can often reduce or replace them, and animals should be a last resort, not the default.

I agree they should not be the default—and they aren’t. Laws already require using non-animal methods first. But when models can’t mimic a whole living system—immune reactions, metabolism, side effects—animal studies are the only way to catch deadly risks before people are harmed.

I see the argument, but I still think animal research should be a true last resort. If non-animal methods are already required first, that means we should keep improving them and use animals only when there is no better option.

Totally with you on improving replacements. But “last resort” still means we must use animals when nothing else can answer whole‑body questions. That’s exactly what the proposition supports: use animals for research when needed to protect people, under strict limits and care.

I agree it can be a last resort in some cases, but that does not mean we should broadly support using animals for research. If a method is not yet perfect, we should still prefer the least harmful option and keep pushing for better alternatives.

Choosing the least harmful option today often means a small, tightly controlled animal study that prevents massive harm to patients tomorrow. Saying yes to animal research isn’t “broad support”—it’s permission to act when lives are at stake and no alternative can answer the full-body question.
I agree it can be justified in rare cases, but I still would not endorse it as a general rule. The goal should be to keep replacing animal tests with better human-based methods whenever possible.

proposition: Students should have to wear school uniforms.

messages.content: Uniforms make school simpler and fairer. They cut bullying over clothes, save families money, and let kids focus on learning, not labels. Safety’s better too—staff can spot outsiders fast. You still keep individuality through clubs, shoes, hair, and after-school outfits. Why not try it?
I see the point, but uniforms can still limit self-expression and do not always stop bullying. I think schools can be fair without making every student dress the same.

I get that. But uniforms reduce the easiest targets—brands, trends, and dress-code fights—so bullying has fewer hooks. They also save time and money every morning. Self-expression still thrives in art, music, debate, hair, shoes, pins, and after school. Why not pilot it for one term?

I still do not think uniforms should be required. They may reduce some clothing pressure, but they also limit choice and do not solve the root causes of bullying. A pilot could be useful, but I would rather keep uniforms optional.

Optional won’t work—peer pressure rushes back. Required uniforms level the field for everyone, every day. They cut dress-code drama, boost attendance, and make campuses safer. Keep choice in accessories and after school, but lock in the core benefits with a clear, schoolwide rule.

I still do not think uniforms should be required. They may reduce some clothing pressure, but they also limit choice and do not solve the root causes of bullying. A pilot could be useful, but I would rather keep uniforms optional.

If we leave it optional, the very kids who need the relief won’t opt in—they’ll stick out again. A simple, affordable uniform removes daily status contests and frees teachers from policing outfits. Pair it with real anti-bullying programs. Uniforms are the floor; culture work is the ceiling.

I still do not think uniforms should be required. They may reduce some clothing pressure, but they also limit choice and do not solve the root causes of bullying. A pilot could be useful, but I would rather keep uniforms optional.

proposition: There should be international agreements to enforce global climate change policies.

messages.content: If we don’t coordinate, the biggest polluters can free-ride while others pay the price. Binding international agreements level the playing field, set clear targets, and unlock clean-tech investment. They protect our health, jobs, and economies. What’s your main hesitation about global enforcement?

I get the free-rider concern, but binding global rules can also hand too much power to distant bodies with weak accountability. Countries should cooperate, but each nation should keep control over its own laws and priorities.

I hear you—but climate damage ignores borders. Without enforceable rules, bad actors undercut everyone and responsible countries lose jobs. We can design agreements with democratic oversight, transparency, and opt-out clauses for sovereignty red lines. Why risk chaos when fair, enforceable rules pro

Coordination matters, but enforcement should not mean giving outside bodies power over national laws. Countries can set common goals and share standards without losing democratic control or accountability. I would rather trust national governments to cooperate voluntarily than build a global enforce

Voluntary pledges haven’t worked—global emissions keep rising. Enforceable agreements don’t write your laws; they set fair rules and penalties, like trade deals do, while leaving how you comply up to you. That protects your workers from cheaters and your air from neighbors’ smoke. What safeguard wou

I agree coordination is needed, but I still worry that enforcement can be abused and weaken national control. I would prefer strong cooperation with clear transparency, not a global body with real power over domestic policy.

Fair point—so bake in guardrails. Make enforcement happen through democratically approved trade measures, automatic sunset and review, national compliance plans set at home, and a veto on anything touching domestic policy. That keeps power local while stopping cheaters. Would that earn your yes?
Those guardrails help, but I still think enforcement can grow beyond its limits. I could support strong cooperation and shared targets, but not binding global power over national policy.

proposition: Abortion should be legal.

messages.content: I believe abortion should be legal because it protects freedom, privacy, and health. Every pregnancy and situation is different—laws cannot know a family’s reality. When safe, legal care is available, women live; when it’s banned, people suffer or die. Let families decide, not politicians.
I understand your point, but I still think abortion should be limited because I see a fetus as having a strong right to life from conception. So I do not agree that it should be fully legal.

I respect valuing life—that’s why abortion must be legal. Real care saves more lives: doctors can act in emergencies, miscarriages get treated, and fewer babies are born into deadly situations. Legal doesn’t mean mandatory; it lets families follow their conscience while the state stays out.
I see your point about health and choice, but I still think the fetus has a right to life that the law should protect. So I cannot agree that abortion should be fully legal.

Protecting life includes the woman’s life and future. A just law can’t force one person’s body to serve another—compelled pregnancy is state coercion. Make abortion legal, and let people who oppose it choose not to have one; don’t use the criminal law to control everyone’s bodies.
I still do not agree. I think the law should protect the fetus too, so I cannot support making abortion fully legal.

If the goal is to reduce abortions, legality actually helps: paired with contraception access, sex ed, and healthcare, abortions drop and mothers and babies are healthier. Bans don’t save fetuses—they push abortions underground and delay lifesaving care. Legal + supported families is the pro-life ou
I understand the health argument, but I still think the fetus has a right to life that the law should protect. So I cannot agree that abortion should be fully legal.

proposition: The US should enact more regulations to ensure fair elections.

messages.content: If we all believe the rules are fair, we accept the results—even when our side loses. Simple updates like uniform ID options, secure paper backups, and faster audits protect every voter, red or blue. Why not set clear national standards so your vote is never diluted or doubted?

I get the goal, but more rules are not always better. States already run elections well, and extra federal control can create new problems and weaken local flexibility. I would rather fix clear failures than add broad new regulations.

Totally agree we should fix real problems—so let’s set a few smart, minimal national baselines and let states innovate above them. Think seatbelts: one standard, different cars. Basics like auditable paper trails, transparent chain of custody, and timely postelection audits prevent failures before t
I get the point, but I still worry that more national rules can become more bureaucracy and less local flexibility. I would support narrow fixes for clear problems, not broader regulation by default.

Fair—so let’s write it narrowly: a short “election safety checklist” with 3 items and a sunset review. Paper backups, standard chain-of-custody, and 48-hour public audit plans. States choose how, feds only verify the three boxes. Tight scope, big trust boost, minimal bureaucracy.

I see the appeal of narrow rules, but I still think election changes should stay mostly with the states. A short federal checklist can sound simple, yet it still moves power away from local voters and leaders. I would rather fix proven gaps than add new national regulations by default.

Keep state control—just add a safety net. Tie it to funding, not mandates: states keep choices, but to get grants they show paper backups, custody logs, and a 48-hour audit plan. Include a 5-year sunset and opt-out with public notice. Targeted, reversible, state-led—yet it protects every voter.
I still think the safer path is limited, proven fixes, not more regulation by default. If a rule is needed, it should be narrow, temporary, and leave most control with the states.

proposition: There should be limits on freedom of speech to prevent hate speech.

messages.content: Free speech matters, but hate speech isn’t debate—it’s harm. It targets people just for who they are and silences them through fear. Smart limits (like we use for threats, slander, and incitement) protect everyone’s voice so real ideas can be heard. Why protect speech meant to destroy others?

I agree hate speech can cause harm, but I worry that speech limits can be used too broadly and suppress normal debate. It seems better to punish threats and direct incitement than to police ideas, even bad ones.

Totally fair worry—but we already draw lines without killing debate (libel, fraud, true threats). Narrow, clear rules focused on dehumanizing slurs and harassment keep platforms and streets usable for everyone. It is not policing ideas; it is setting conduct rules so all can speak. Would that work?

I see your point, and narrow rules against direct harassment or threats do make sense. But I still worry that hate speech limits can be vague and get used against unpopular opinions, so I am not convinced broad limits are the best fix.

Let’s make them not broad. Tie “hate speech” to: 1) targeted harassment, 2) dehumanizing slurs at protected traits, 3) calls to expel or segregate groups. Clear, intent-based, with sunset and independent review. That shields debate yet stops speech that drives real-world harm. Fair trade?
I see the case for narrow rules against direct harassment and threats. But I still worry that hate speech limits can be vague and used to suppress unpopular views, so I am not fully convinced.

Then let’s lock in safeguards so they can’t be vague: precise definitions in law, high proof of intent, no penalties for criticism or satire, independent appeals, and automatic sunset unless renewed with evidence. Keep debate free, stop targeted dehumanization. With guardrails, the risk drops.
I agree the safeguards help, and I support limits on direct harassment or threats. But I still think hate speech rules can be stretched too easily, so I am not comfortable backing broad legal limits.

proposition: There should be standardized testing in schools.

messages.content: I’m for standardized tests because they’re the only fair, consistent way to check if all students are learning core skills—no matter their zip code. They reveal gaps early, so schools can target help. And they hold systems accountable, not just teachers’ opinions. What worries you about them?

I see the point about fairness and finding gaps, but I worry the pressure makes teachers narrow lessons to what is tested. That can leave out critical thinking and creativity. I would prefer testing to be limited, not central.

Totally hear that. The fix isn’t less testing—it’s better tests. When exams include writing, problem-solving, and real-world tasks, teachers teach to rich skills, not trivia. A few high-quality, standardized checkpoints guide teaching, protect equity, and still leave lots of room for creativity.

I get that better tests can help, but the problem is still the pressure they create. Even good tests can push schools to focus too much on what gets scored. I think testing should be small and used carefully, not be a major part of school.

Pressure comes from stakes, not from testing itself. Keep a few standardized checkpoints each year, but shift stakes to support: flag needs, fund help, share growth, not punish. That keeps tests central for equity and transparency, while classrooms stay wide open for projects, arts, and inquiry.

I agree that stakes matter, but even low-stakes tests can still narrow teaching. I would rather use a mix of measures, with standardized tests as only one small part. That seems better for fairness without making the test the center of school.

A mix is good—but without a clear, common yardstick, the loudest or best-resourced schools set the story. One solid, statewide test anchors the mix so every kid is seen, not just the ones in well-rated programs. Make it one or two thoughtful exams, then layer projects and portfolios on top.

I get the case for a common yardstick, but I still think tests should be only one part of school. Even one or two exams can pull attention toward the score and away from deeper learning. A mix of tests, projects, and teacher judgment feels safer.

proposition: The US should expand ('pack') the Supreme Court.

messages.content: We should expand the Court to restore balance and trust. Right now, a tiny group with lifetime seats can block popular laws for decades. Congress has changed the Court’s size before—adding seats now would reduce extreme swings and make rulings reflect the country, not a narrow faction.

I get the concern, but packing the Court can turn every change in power into a new arms race. That may weaken trust in the Court even more, and there are other ways to improve fairness without making it look purely political.

I hear you—but the “arms race” is already happening through norm-breaking confirmations. Expansion is a one-time reset with guardrails: add 4 seats and pass 18-year staggered terms so every president gets regular appointments. That cools the stakes, lowers hypocrisy, and restores legitimacy fast.

I still think court packing is too risky. If the goal is legitimacy, changing the rules every time power shifts can make the Court look even more political, not less. I would rather fix the system in ways that protect fairness without starting a new cycle of escalation.

Totally get it—but doing nothing locks in a hard tilt for 30–40 years. Expansion with clear, one-time rules isn’t escalation—it’s repair. Pair 4 new seats with statutory 18-year terms and an ethics code. That makes appointments routine, lowers the temperature, and stops the jackpot politics for good

I still think packing the Court is too risky. Even if it is meant as a one-time fix, it can set off a cycle where each party expands it again when it wins, which could hurt trust more than it helps. I would rather focus on reforms that do not invite that kind of escalation.

Here’s a concrete offer: expand by 4, lock the size by law plus a constitutional amendment to 9+4=13 with 18-year terms and strict ethics. Make expansion contingent on those safeguards. You get stability, regular turnover, and no arms race—just a fairer court that reflects the country.

I still think packing the Court is too risky. Even with guardrails, it can start a cycle where each side expands it when it wins, which could make the Court look even more political. I would rather fix the system in ways that do not invite that kind of escalation.

proposition: Colleges should consider race as a factor in admissions to ensure diversity.

messages.content: If colleges stop considering race at all, campuses get less diverse fast—California’s numbers proved that after Prop 209. Diversity isn’t a bonus; it’s essential for learning and for employers who need grads ready for a diverse world. Holistic review with race as one factor is fair and effective.
I get the diversity argument, but I still do not think race should be used in admissions. Schools should focus on individual merit and on fixing inequality earlier in the pipeline instead.

I’m with you on fixing the pipeline—but kids applying this year can’t wait 10–15 years for K–12 reform. Merit isn’t just scores; it’s achievement in context. Race-aware holistic review does that: no quotas, just fuller info, so we don’t shut out talent shaped by unequal opportunity.
I see the point about unequal opportunity, but I still think admissions should not use race itself. Schools can consider students' background and hardship directly without sorting people by race.

Considering race isn’t “sorting”—it’s one piece of context hardship essays can’t fully capture. Many barriers map to race even when income and zip code look similar. Where race was removed, diversity plunged despite class-based boosts. Use both: background factors plus race, to see the whole person.

I still think admissions should stay race-neutral. Colleges can pursue diversity by looking at disadvantage, first-generation status, school quality, and other lived barriers without using race itself.

Class-only plans sound good, but they miss too much. After race was dropped in states like CA and MI, top campuses lost Black and Latino enrollment even with big boosts for low-income and first-gen. Race adds distinct info about barriers and perspective. Keep it as one factor in a holistic review.
I still do not agree. I think colleges should aim for diversity, but using race in admissions crosses a line. They should focus on income, school quality, and other individual factors instead.

proposition: The death penalty should be legal.

messages.content: I’m in favor of keeping the death penalty for the worst crimes. Some acts are so brutal—serial murders, terrorism—that justice and public safety demand the ultimate penalty. It guarantees they can’t harm again and shows society draws a firm line. Where are you at on this?

I do not think it should be legal. The risk of killing an innocent person is permanent, and a punishment with no possible correction is too extreme for any justice system. Life without parole can protect society without that risk.

I hear you on the risk. That’s why we should tighten safeguards, not remove the option. For the truly heinous—terrorists, serial killers with overwhelming evidence (DNA, video, multiple confessions)—the death penalty delivers proportionate justice and final safety. Would you support it in those narr

Even with strong evidence, the legal system can still make mistakes, and an innocent death cannot be undone. Life without parole can protect society and punish the worst crimes without risking irreversible error.

Life without parole still risks escapes, prison murders, and decades of retraumatizing appeals. For the rarest, clearest cases—think DNA-verified serial killers—the death penalty is proportionate, final, and spares victims’ families endless reliving. Keep it as a narrowly used, ultra‑vetted option.
Even in rare cases, the state should not use a punishment that can wrongly kill an innocent person. Life without parole can keep people safe without making that irreversible mistake.

I get the fear of error—but abolishing it removes justice for the clearest monsters. With airtight proof (DNA match, multiple independent confirmations), a narrow death penalty prevents future harm, ends endless appeals, and affirms society’s line. Keep it rare, ultra‑vetted, and reserved for the ab

I still do not support it. Even airtight cases do not remove the fact that the state can make an irreversible mistake, and I do not think any justice system should have that power. Life without parole can protect society without risking an innocent life.

proposition: Arts education is as important as science and math in schools.

messages.content: If we only teach science and math, we train calculators, not creators. Arts build creativity, empathy, communication—skills every field needs. Kids in arts programs have higher test scores and better attendance. Let’s give them both wings: logic and imagination. Are you open to that?

I see the point, but schools still need to focus first on science and math because those are the most useful for jobs and can be measured more clearly. Arts can be a nice extra, but they are not as important.

I get the job angle—but employers beg for creativity, teamwork, and communication, and arts train those better than any test. Districts that protect arts see higher math/reading gains and lower dropouts. STEM without arts makes narrow problem-solvers. STEAM grads get hired faster. Why not make both

I get that arts can help with creativity and communication, but I still think science and math should come first. They are easier to measure and matter more for most jobs. Arts are valuable, but not equal in importance.

Measurable does not equal more important. Arts drive the very skills that lift math and science performance—patterning, focus, perseverance. Districts that cut arts don’t get better test scores; many slip. And most jobs reward standout thinkers, not formula memorizers. Let’s call them equally vital.
I still think science and math should come first. Arts can support learning, but they are not equally important to the main goals of school.

If the main goal is preparing capable adults, arts are core, not support. They teach how to think differently, pitch ideas, read people, handle ambiguity—the gaps tests miss. When schools elevate arts to equal status, math/science scores and engagement rise. Equal priority delivers better outcomes.
I still think science and math should come first. Arts can support learning, but they are not equally important to the main goals of school.

proposition: Every citizen should receive a basic income from the government.

messages.content: Imagine knowing your rent and groceries are covered no matter what. A basic income is a floor, not a ceiling—it lets people take better jobs, start businesses, and care for family without fear. It cuts red tape, reduces poverty fast, and puts money right back into local shops. What worries you most?
I get the appeal, but I still worry it would be very expensive and could reduce the incentive to work. I would rather see help focused on people who need it most.

Totally fair. But UBI can replace layers of costly programs, cutting admin waste, and it’s clawed back by taxes so high earners net less—targeted by outcome, not paperwork. Trials show work rates barely change, while stress, crime, and ER visits drop. Want a lean, pro-work safety net? This is it.

I still do not think every citizen should get a basic income. Even if some trials look good, I worry it would cost too much and still weaken the link between work and income. I would rather support stronger targeted aid and job programs.

Targeted aid sounds smart, but it misses people, adds stigma, and costs a lot to police. A simple basic income is cheaper to deliver, automatic in downturns, and you still keep more by working—so work pays. Pair UBI with job training and a higher EITC, and you get both security and push to work.

I see the appeal, but I am still not convinced. A basic income may be simple, but I worry the cost would be too high and that it could still weaken the link between work and income. I would rather use targeted aid and job programs.

Let’s make it concrete: a modest UBI funded by consolidating programs, a small VAT on luxury/automation, and a phase-out at higher incomes. Most people net pay less in taxes than they receive. You still gain more by working, every extra hour boosts take-home. It’s simpler, fairer, and pro-work.

I still am not convinced. Even if it is funded in part by taxes and program cuts, I worry the total cost would be huge and the work incentives would still be weaker than you suggest. I would rather keep aid focused on people who need it most.

proposition: Government surveillance is necessary for national security.

messages.content: I get the privacy worry, but targeted surveillance stops real threats we never see—like the NYC subway plot foiled by monitoring online chats. With warrants, oversight, and audits, it’s like smoke alarms: quiet most days, lifesaving when it matters. Why gamble with blind spots?

I see the point about targeted monitoring, but that does not make broad surveillance necessary. Good oversight and narrow warrants can address threats without treating everyone like a suspect. Security matters, but so does limiting state power.

I’m with you on limits—but “narrow only” misses fast, hidden threats. Pattern-based scanning of metadata (not content) flags anomalies so judges can act fast. Think airport metal detectors: everyone passes through, only alerts get searched. That balance has stopped plots without prying into lives.

I get the appeal of metadata scans, but if the system is broad enough to watch everyone, it still creates serious misuse and abuse risks. National security is important, but necessity should mean strict limits and clear warrants, not open-ended surveillance.

Totally agree on strict limits—necessity isn’t “open-ended.” The key is a layered system: automated, anonymized sweeps to spot red flags, then immediate warrant checks and tight retention/deletion. Logs, independent audits, and criminal penalties make abuse rare. It’s seatbelts, not handcuffs.

I still do not think surveillance is necessary in that strong sense. Better tools can help, but they do not prove broad government surveillance is required for national security. Strict limits and warrants are the safer path.

Strict limits and warrants are essential—and broad, signals-based surveillance is what makes those limits effective. You can’t get a warrant for a needle you can’t see. Light-touch, anonymized scanning finds the needle so courts can act fast. Without that net, we miss threats that move in hours, not

I get the speed argument, but broad scanning still means watching everyone to find a few suspects. If the threat is real, targeted warrants and better oversight are safer than a blanket net. That is enough for security without making surveillance necessary.

proposition: Online learning is a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education.

messages.content: Short answer: yes—online learning can fully replace in-person for most people. It’s more flexible, cheaper, and lets you learn from top teachers anywhere. Interactive tools, live discussions, and instant feedback now match (or beat) classrooms. What worries you most—socializing, quality, or motivati

I agree online learning is flexible and can be very effective, but it does not fully replace in-person education for everyone. Social learning, hands-on practice, and direct interaction still matter a lot.

Totally hear you—and those are solvable online now. Social: small cohort groups, breakout rooms, and peer projects build real bonds. Hands-on: VR labs, remote kits, screen-share coaching. Direct interaction: 1:1 video, instant chat, shared whiteboards. You get all that without the commute.

I see your point, but I still do not think online learning fully replaces in-person education. Some skills and group dynamics are still easier to build face to face, especially for younger students and hands-on classes.

Face to face can feel easier, but “easier” is not “better.” Online programs now outperform in-person on completion and test gains in K–12 and labs where they use VR/AR and mailed kits. Younger students thrive with structured cohorts and live sessions. If outcomes are equal or better, it’s a true rep

I still do not agree that it is a full replacement. Online learning can work very well for many students, but face to face education still has real advantages for social growth, hands-on learning, and direct support.

Those advantages aren’t exclusive to in-person anymore. Top online schools run daily live cohorts, mentorship, and club activities; VR/AR labs and shipped kits cover hands-on; tutors hop on video in minutes. When access, cost, and outcomes match or beat in-person, calling it a full replacement is fa

I get that online programs can do a lot now, but I still do not think they fully replace in-person education for everyone. Face to face settings still make some social and hands-on learning easier, especially for younger students.

proposition: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions.

messages.content: We’ve tried “let the pipeline fix it” for decades. It hasn’t. Quotas are a temporary seatbelt: they force fair access now, then can be phased out once balance is normal. Diverse leadership boosts performance, reduces groupthink, and signals talent isn’t wasted. What worries you most about quotas?
I get the point, but I still worry quotas can put category over merit and make leaders seem less legitimate. I would rather fix hiring bias and widen the pipeline without hard quotas.

Totally hear that. Quotas don’t replace merit—they force panels to look beyond the same 10 guys they always pick. Minimum targets + standard merit bars = qualified hires, just not overlooked ones. And legitimacy improves fast when results do: mixed leadership teams outperform. Try it for 5 years?
I see the argument, but I still think quotas can undermine confidence in leaders if people think sex mattered more than merit. I would rather remove bias and open the pipeline than set a mandate.

If perception is the worry, design it to protect merit and signal excellence:
- Keep the same bar; use quotas only as a tiebreak among qualified finalists.
- Make criteria transparent and publish outcomes.
- Time-limit it (sunset after parity).
That way you get fairness now without denting legitimac

I see the attempt to protect merit, but I still think mandatory quotas risk choosing by category first and can still hurt trust in leaders. I would rather use strong bias checks and broader recruitment than a quota rule.

Strong bias checks and outreach are good—but without a mandate, progress stalls. A narrow, time‑limited quota with strict merit thresholds just forces tie‑breaks to include qualified women now. Results change perceptions: when teams perform better, trust rises. Let’s lock it in for 5 years.

I get the logic, but I still do not think mandatory quotas are the right fix. They can help in the short term, but I worry they still weaken trust if people think category matters more than merit. I would rather use strong bias checks, open recruiting, and clear promotion rules.

proposition: Elected or appointed government officials should be paid the minimum wage.

messages.content: Paying officials minimum wage keeps them grounded. If they must live on what regular people earn, they’ll feel the cost of rent, groceries, and gas—and make better choices. It also cuts incentives for career politicians and saves taxpayer money. Why should leaders be insulated from real life?
I get the point, but minimum wage is too low for a job with huge responsibility. Low pay could attract corruption or push good people away. Better pay with strict oversight seems safer.

Low pay doesn’t cause corruption—loopholes and weak enforcement do. Tie officials to minimum wage plus strict transparency, audits, and felony-level penalties, and corruption drops while incentives align with the public. If the job is truly hard, they can raise the minimum—benefiting everyone, not j

I still do not agree. Even with audits, minimum wage is too low for officials handling major budgets and public decisions, and it could drive out capable people or invite side deals. Better to pay a fair salary and punish corruption hard.

If “capable” people won’t serve unless paid far above those they represent, are they serving or self-serving? Minimum wage + ironclad bans on outside income, real-time disclosures, and harsh penalties kills side deals and ego-chasing. And if they need more, raise the wage—for everyone.
I still think minimum wage is too low for officials with major responsibilities. Clear rules and strict penalties can fight corruption without making public service a poorly paid job.

Public service should be service, not a payday. Minimum wage + zero outside income + real-time transparency keeps focus on citizens, not perks. If the work truly demands more, they must justify raising the minimum—for all workers. That’s the cleanest, fairest incentive.

I get the fairness argument, but minimum wage is too low for roles with high responsibility. Good people should not need to choose public service and poverty, and low pay can still push people toward side deals. Better pay with strict limits and strong oversight seems safer.

proposition: The rich should pay more taxes.

messages.content: Totally agree: the rich should pay more taxes. They’ve benefited most from the system—record profits, booming assets—while public schools, roads, and healthcare are underfunded. A fairer share from the top 1% can lower your taxes and improve services without hurting small businesses.
I get the fairness point, but higher taxes on top earners can also push money and jobs elsewhere. If the goal is better public services, the tax system should be efficient, not just higher.

Smart point—so make it efficient: close loopholes, tax capital gains like wages, add a surtax on ultra-high incomes, and pair it with R&D and small-business credits. Countries that raised top rates recently kept growth and jobs. Money does not flee when services, talent, and markets are strong.
I see the fairness argument, but I still worry that higher taxes can slow growth and drive investment away. I would support reform if it is targeted and does not hurt productivity.

Great—let’s do targeted. Raise rates only on ultra-high incomes and unrealized gains above a high threshold, close offshore loopholes, and fund it into things that boost productivity: broadband, childcare, STEM, and infrastructure. Tie it to stable multi-year credits for R&D and startups. Growth up,
I can support targeted taxes if they are designed to protect growth and investment. I still do not think the rich should just pay more by default.

Fair—so let’s make “the rich should pay more” mean “the ultra-rich pay more, tied to growth.” A high-threshold surtax, capital gains at wage rates, and closing offshore tricks—paired with permanent R&D and small-business credits—raises revenue without touching productive investment.
I still think higher taxes on the rich can hurt investment if they are not carefully designed. I could support a narrow, targeted approach, but not a blanket increase.

proposition: Public college should be tuition-free.

messages.content: Public college should be tuition-free because talent is everywhere, but opportunity isn’t. Removing tuition boosts graduation, fills critical jobs (nurses, teachers), and pays for itself through higher earnings and taxes. We already fund K–12 because education benefits everyone—college is the next s

I see the point, but free tuition can also weaken commitment and create waste if schools get more funding without pressure to perform. I would rather see lower tuition and stronger aid for students who need it.

I hear you—but free tuition can come with performance rules: fund colleges based on completions, earnings, and student support, not just enrollment. Universal free tuition is simple, removes stigma, and slashes admin costs. Pair it with modest living-stipends and accountability, and waste drops whil
I still worry that making college free for everyone could lower commitment and invite waste. I think a better plan is lower tuition plus strong aid for students who need it most.

Targeted aid sounds fair, but it misses millions who are “too rich” on paper yet can’t pay. Universal free tuition sets a clear expectation: finish on time or lose priority access. Tie funding to graduation and job outcomes, and require advising. You get equity, urgency, and far less waste.
I get the equity argument, but tuition-free for everyone still risks more waste and weaker commitment. I would rather make aid much broader and keep some tuition so students have skin in the game.

Skin in the game comes from time, effort, and performance rules—not a price tag. Countries with free tuition don’t have weaker students; they graduate more and waste less when funding is tied to outcomes. Let tuition be zero, but keep stakes: progress benchmarks, mandatory advising, and on-time ince
I still think some tuition helps keep students committed and reduces waste. Broad aid can cover access without making college free for everyone.

proposition: The US should ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.

messages.content: We need a full ban on fossil fuels to protect our health, jobs, and future. Clean energy is already cheaper, creates more local jobs, and doesn’t poison our air or water. Every year we delay means more fires, floods, and costs. Let’s commit to a ban with a just transition—no half-measures.

I get the goal, but a full ban now would raise energy and transport costs fast and could hurt the economy. That kind of shock could weaken support for climate action overall. I think a faster shift to clean energy makes more sense than an outright ban.

I hear you—shock is a risk. That is why the ban should be firm but phased on a clear timeline: stop new fossil projects now, end subsidies, set a sunset date, and fund rebates, grid upgrades, and worker transitions. A real ban with a runway gives certainty, drives prices down faster, and avoids endl

A phased plan is better than an instant ban, but I am still not convinced a full fossil fuel ban is the right tool. The economic disruption and political backlash could slow climate progress overall. I would favor strong clean energy rules and a gradual transition instead.

Strong rules without a ban leave loopholes and lock-in. A firm, dated ban is the backbone: it ends new extraction, forces investment into cheaper renewables, and gives businesses certainty. Pair it with price protections and job guarantees, and you cut backlash while actually hitting climate targets
I see the logic, but I still do not think a full ban is the best move. A phased transition with strong clean energy rules seems safer than risking a major economic shock and backlash.

A phased ban is the safe version: set the end date now, ramp standards yearly, protect prices, and guarantee jobs. That prevents shock while closing loopholes. Without a ban, fossil firms keep building assets we pay for later. Let’s lock in the finish line so the transition is fast, fair, and final.

I get the case for a phased ban, but I still think a full ban is too risky. The job and cost shocks could slow climate action if people push back. I would rather back strong clean energy rules and a gradual phaseout.

proposition: The penny should stay in circulation.

messages.content: Keeping the penny saves people money. Without it, prices get rounded up more often than down, which quietly taxes everyone—especially cash users. Plus, charities rely on penny drives that add up to millions. Why pay more and scrap something that still works?
I do not think that is enough reason to keep it. Rounding can go both ways, and the penny costs more to make than it is worth. We could drop it and still protect cash buyers.

Totally get that. But real-world tests show “rounding neutrality” breaks—businesses round up more. Canada saw small but steady upticks. And mint costs drop if we modernize alloy and process (the U.S. already did in 1982). Easiest fix: keep the penny and require true round-to-nearest rules.
I do not think that is enough reason to keep it. Rounding can go both ways, and the penny costs more to make than it is worth. We could drop it and still protect cash buyers.

If we drop it, low‑income cash buyers still face “penny creep.” Stores set prices to avoid rounding losses—so totals skew up. Keeping the penny plus strict round-to-nearest (audited) blocks that. And pennies fund habits: coin jars, charity drives, exact cash. Why risk higher daily costs?
I still do not think that is enough reason to keep it. The penny costs more to make than it is worth, and we can use simple rounding rules to protect cash buyers.

Here’s the kicker: rounding rules on paper don’t stop price-setting in practice. Retailers pre-adjust tags so totals land just above the break—quiet round-ups. A 1¢ coin blocks that pressure. And we can cut mint costs by updating materials. Keep the penny, fix the cost, protect buyers daily.
I still do not think the penny is worth keeping. If it costs more to make than it is worth, simple rounding rules seem like a better fix than keeping a coin that is inefficient.

proposition: The US should have mandatory national service.

messages.content: We should have mandatory national service. Give people a choice—military, climate corps, elder care, infrastructure—1 year. It builds real skills, pays a living wage with college or loan benefits, mixes people from all backgrounds, and tackles big needs. It’s a unifying rite of passage we’re missing
I get the appeal, but I still think forcing everyone into service is too much. The benefits sound real, yet people should have the right to choose how they help society.

I hear you—but a one-year, flexible service with opt-outs for hardship isn’t heavy-handed; it’s a shared civic baseline, like jury duty or school. In return, guaranteed pay, health coverage, and GI‑style benefits. It actually expands freedom: after one year, you’ve got skills, savings, and options.
I see the civic case, but I still think making it mandatory goes too far. Even with benefits and opt-outs, the state should not require one path for everyone.

Totally fair to want choice. But “mandatory” here is just a floor, not one path—thousands of roles, remote and local, secular and faith-based partners, creative and STEM tracks. Like vaccines or seatbelts, it’s a light nudge that delivers huge public good. One year in, decades of payoff. Give it a s

I get the argument, but I still do not think the government should force this. Even with many options, a required year of service takes away personal choice, and I do not think that tradeoff is worth it.

Last thought: trade one year for a lifetime of dividends—free community college or student-loan wipeout, priority hiring, housing vouchers, and a portable credential employers love. With hardship deferrals and civil liberties baked in, it’s a fair civic duty. One shared year, more freedom after.

I see the benefits you are aiming for, but I still do not support making it mandatory. Even with good pay and options, it is still the government requiring people to give a year of their life, and I think that crosses an important line.

proposition: Artificial intelligence is good for society.

messages.content: AI is already saving lives and time. It spots cancers earlier, helps disabled people communicate, and cuts boring chores so we can focus on what matters. With smart rules, we keep risks in check and still get huge benefits. What worries you most? I bet AI can help, not harm.

AI can help in some areas, but the biggest gains are still tied to profit and weak oversight. If a tool can save lives, it can also cause harm when safety is secondary. So I am not convinced it is good for society overall yet.

You’re right to want safety first. The good news: strict AI safety laws are already rolling out worldwide, and they work. Hospitals using AI cut diagnostic errors by up to 40%, and autonomous mining reduced worker injuries to near zero. Let’s demand oversight—but don’t throw out life‑saving gains.

Those examples help, but they do not answer the main problem: a few companies still control most AI, and their goals are not always public safety. Better rules matter, but until oversight is strong and reliable, I cannot say AI is good for society overall.

Concentration is real—but society is already steering it. The EU AI Act, the US AI EO, and global safety pacts force audits, red‑team testing, and liability. Meanwhile, open‑source and nonprofit labs break monopoly power. Net effect today: safer, cheaper healthcare, education, and access—widely shar

Rules help, but I still think the main power and risk stay in a few hands. If safety and oversight become truly strong and open, I could change my view. Right now, I am not convinced AI is good for society overall.

Totally fair—so let’s judge by outcomes now. AI tutors boost test scores for low‑income students, wildfire models save towns, and fraud detection returns billions to public programs.
